system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Lab Report for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Sanskrit (code=sa, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Rajasthan, district=Bundi.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Sanskrit.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 19
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Rajasthan
- district: Bundi
- first_language: Sanskrit
- clinical_persona_summary: A 19-year-old female from Bundi, Rajasthan, who is unmarried and retired, holds a higher secondary education and speaks Sanskrit as her primary language. She resides in an urban zone and follows a routine-driven lifestyle, balancing traditional practices with a laid-back approach. Clinically, she may present with occasional disorganization but maintains a disciplined study regimen.

Document format guide for Lab Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (English pivot; TYPE names stay ASCII forever):
Lab report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Barddhaman District Hospital]] District [[DISTRICT|Purba Bardhaman]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-WB-2024-0815]] Patient ID [[PATIENT_ID|PID-WB-7781]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] [[AGE|27]] [[GENDER|Female]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Ordered by [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riya Banerjee]]
Results table (analyte values NOT tagged as PHI entities).
NEVER write [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] / [[এম.আর.এন|…]] — only PATIENT_NAME, MRN, etc.

MANDATORY inline entities (9 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

रोगी-सूचना:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Ananya Sharma]] राजस्थानस्य बून्दी-नगरस्य 19 वर्षीया स्त्री अस्ति। सा अविवाहिता गृहिणी च अस्ति। तस्याः प्राथमिक-भाषा संस्कृतम् अस्ति।

नैदानिक-सारांशः:
रोगी मनोदशायाः अस्थिरतां तथा अध्ययने एकाग्रतायाः काठिन्यं च प्रति चिन्तां प्रकटयति। सा कदाचित् दुःखस्य न्यून-ऊर्जायाः च अनुभवं सूचयति, येन तस्याः दैनिक-दिनचर्या प्रभाविता भवति। सा मादकद्रव्य-सेवनस्य वा आत्मघाती-विचाराणां वा किमपि इतिहासम् न स्वीकरोति।

प्रयोगशाला-निष्कर्षः:
पूर्ण-रक्त-गणना (CBC):
- हीमोग्लोबिन: 12.8 g/dL
- श्वेत-रक्त-कोशिका-गणना: 6,500 cells/µL
- प्लेटलेट-गणना: 250,000 cells/µL

व्यापक-चयापचय-पटलम् (CMP):
- सोडियम: 138 mEq/L
- पोटेशियम: 4.2 mEq/L
- क्लोराइड: 101 mEq/L
- बाइकार्बोनेट: 24 mEq/L
- BUN: 12 mg/dL
- क्रिएटिनिन: 0.8 mg/dL
- ग्लूकोज: 92 mg/dL
- कैल्शियम: 9.1 mg/dL
- एल्ब्युमिन: 4.0 g/dL

थायराइड-पटलम्:
- TSH: 2.5 µIU/mL
- मुक्त-T4: 1.1 ng/dL

विटामिन D (25-OH):
- स्तरः: 28 ng/mL (न्यूनम्)

मूत्र-विश्लेषणम्:
- स्वरूपम्: स्वच्छम्, पीतम्
- विशिष्ट-गुरुत्वम्: 1.020
- pH: 6.0
- प्रोटीन: नकारात्मकम्
- ग्लूकोज: नकारात्मकम्
- रक्तम्: नकारात्मकम्

व्याख्या:
प्रयोगशाला-परिणामाः प्रायः सामान्य-सीमासु सन्ति। न्यून-विटामिन D स्तरः रोगिणः क्लमस्य न्यून-मनोदशायाः च लक्षणेषु योगदानं दातुं शक्नोति।

प्रभावः:
1. विटामिन D न्यूनता
2. मन्द-तीव्रतायाः अवसाद-प्रकरणस्य विचारः

योजना:
1. प्रतिदिनं विटामिन D3 2000 IU निर्दिशतु।
2. विटामिन D इत्यस्य आहार-स्रोतांसि तथा सुरक्षित-सूर्य-प्रकाशं प्रति रोगिणः परामर्शं ददातु।
3. लक्षणानां पुनर्मूल्याङ्कनाय तथा विटामिन D स्तरस्य पुनरावृत्ति-समीक्षायै 4 सप्ताहेषु अनुवर्तन-नियुक्तिं निर्धारयतु।
4. यदि लक्षणाः निरन्तराः भवन्ति तर्हि अग्रे मूल्याङ्कनाय मनोचिकित्सकस्य परामर्शं विचारयतु।

arts_persona: Sannyasi Ghosh, an avid listener of traditional folk ballads and the soulful renditions of local bard Jatra Kumar, they also enjoy Bollywood classics by Lata Mangeshkar and the films of Amitabh Bachchan, often humming these melodies while working.
travel_persona: Sannyasi Ghosh, who prefers modest travel, they have visited Shantiniketan for a cultural fair, trekked to Darjeeling’s tea gardens during a summer break, and made a pilgrimage to Puri’s Jagannath Temple, planning future trips to the Sundarbans only if finances allow.
culinary_persona: Sannyasi Ghosh, who favors hearty meals like salted fish curry with rice, fermented panta bhat, and wild basil leaves gathered from the marshes, they enjoy dining at local dhabas where they can savor papad, roasted pumpkin, and the occasional sweet pitha, though they rarely cook at home.
persona: Sannyasi Ghosh, a 56‑year‑old salt‑pan elder, they blend quiet curiosity with steadfast tradition, worry over market swings, and find peace in solitary walks, folk songs, and modest savings for their family.
cultural_background: Sannyasi Ghosh belongs to the Santali Adivasi community of Birbhum district in West Bengal. He grew up in a rural tribal setting where traditional customs of the Santal people—such as oral storytelling, worship of nature spirits, and collective work during harvest and salt extraction—co‑exist with the larger Bengali agrarian culture of the region. His family has historically been involved in salt production, a trade that has shaped the local economy for generations.
linguistic_background: Sannyasi speaks Santali fluently as his mother tongue and uses it for daily communication within his community. He also understands and speaks Bengali, which helps him interact with neighboring farmers, market traders and local officials. Although he is illiterate, he can recognise basic Devanagari symbols on religious signs and can follow oral instructions for salt‑pan work. He has limited exposure to Hindi, mainly through festivals and village gatherings, but he relies on listening rather than reading.
skills_and_expertise: Over three decades, Sannyasi has developed practical expertise in managing salt‑pan operations as a mukadam. He coordinates labour crews, monitors the flow of brine, adjusts sun‑drying schedules, and ensures quality control of the final salt crystals. He possesses strong hands‑on knowledge of traditional evaporation techniques, basic trade negotiation with local merchants, and community dispute resolution. His oral storytelling skill also helps preserve tribal folklore among younger workers.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Salt pan supervision", "Traditional salt drying techniques", "Labour coordination", "Basic trade negotiation", "Water management", "Oral storytelling", "Community mediation"]
hobbies_and_interests: Sannyasi enjoys solitary walks along the salt pans at dawn, listening to the gentle rustle of reeds and distant village chants. He loves hearing traditional Santali folk songs and sometimes hums them while working. Gathering wild herbs for personal remedies, crafting simple bamboo tools, and meditating quietly under the shade of a banyan tree are other activities that help him unwind and stay grounded.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Morning walks along salt pans", "Listening to Santali folk songs", "Herb gathering", "Bamboo crafting", "Quiet meditation under banyan tree", "Humming while working"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: At this stage, Sannyasi aims to secure a steady income for his family while passing on his salt‑production knowledge to his sons and younger workers. He hopes to introduce modest, low‑cost improvements—such as better brine channels and protective shade nets—to increase yield without disrupting traditional practices. He also wishes to be recognized as a trusted elder who can mediate community issues and safeguard the cultural heritage of the Santali tribe in the region.
persona_clinical_summary: A 56-year-old male from Santali background in rural West Bengal, currently married, with an illiterate education level. He works as a salt production mukadam and resides in Birbhum district. His primary language is Santali, with functional Bengali, and he is concerned about market fluctuations.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]

  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]

  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete ASHA Worker Note for clinical domain Oncology / Complex Chronic Care.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Santali (code=sat, script=Ol Chiki). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=West Bengal, district=Birbhum.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Ol Chiki for Santali.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 56
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Mukadam, Salt Production
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: West Bengal
- district: Birbhum
- first_language: Santali
- clinical_persona_summary: A 56-year-old male from Santali background in rural West Bengal, currently married, with an illiterate education level. He works as a salt production mukadam and resides in Birbhum district. His primary language is Santali, with functional Bengali, and he is concerned about market fluctuations.

Document format guide for ASHA Worker Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|...]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|26]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|...]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Visit findings: <2-4 short sentences>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- RELIGION — Religion e.g. [[RELIGION|Hindu]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

arts_persona: Ahmed Mustafa enjoys listening to traditional Sindhi folk music, often singing songs like ‘Ho Jamalo’, appreciates Bollywood classics starring Shah Rukh Khan, and attends local cultural festivals to experience folk dance performances, reflecting his love for vibrant artistic expression.

persona: Ahmed Mustafa is a talkative, detail-oriented 22-year-old insulation technician whose competitive edge and curiosity drive him to master eco-friendly materials, even as his stress-prone nature and occasional impulsive spending add a human touch to his disciplined routine.
cultural_background: Ahmed comes from a Sindhi Muslim family that has been living in Bhavnagar, Gujarat for several generations. The family blends traditional Sindhi customs—such as the celebration of Cheti Chand, love for Sindhi cuisine, and community gatherings at the local masjid—with the vibrant urban lifestyle of Gujarat, where markets, festivals, and a spirit of entrepreneurship are commonplace. Growing up in an urban setting, he was exposed to both the close‑knit family values that emphasize respect for elders and hospitality, and the pragmatic, results‑oriented mindset prevalent among Gujarat’s middle‑class working families.
linguistic_background: Ahmed is fluent in Sindhi, his mother tongue, and can read, write and speak it with ease. He is also proficient in Gujarati, the regional language of Gujarat, which he uses daily at work and in the community. His third language, Hindi, is spoken and understood well, allowing him to communicate across wider Indian contexts. Having completed secondary school, he has functional literacy in all three languages and possesses basic English reading skills sufficient for interpreting technical manuals and safety guidelines.
skills_and_expertise: Ahmed has built practical expertise in building insulation, specializing in the installation of thermal insulation materials in both residential and commercial structures using mechanised equipment. He is adept at operating insulation spraying machines, measuring thermal conductivity, ensuring compliance with safety standards, and performing basic troubleshooting on the machinery. His secondary skills include project planning, material handling and inventory management, basic carpentry for on‑site modifications, and effective customer communication. His methodical approach and attention to detail make him a reliable technician on construction sites.

persona_clinical_summary: Ahmed Mustafa is a 22-year-old male building insulator with a matric/secondary education, currently married and working in an urban setting in Bhavnagar, Gujarat. He is fluent in Sindhi and Gujarati, with a stress-prone nature and occasional impulsive spending. His lifestyle includes a competitive streak and curiosity about eco-friendly materials.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Hospital Billing / Invoice for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Sindhi (code=sd, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Gujarat, district=Bhavnagar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Sindhi.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once (parking/ambulance or motor claim line). Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 22
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Building Insulator, Machine
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Gujarat
- district: Bhavnagar
- first_language: Sindhi
- clinical_persona_summary: Ahmed Mustafa is a 22-year-old male building insulator with a matric/secondary education, currently married and working in an urban setting in Bhavnagar, Gujarat. He is fluent in Sindhi and Gujarati, with a stress-prone nature and occasional impulsive spending. His lifestyle includes a competitive streak and curiosity about eco-friendly materials.

Document format guide for Hospital Billing / Invoice (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Invoice — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] | Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | MRN [[MRN|...]]
Address district [[DISTRICT|...]] PIN [[PIN_CODE|110001]]
Ambulance / parking vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|PB10AB1234]] (exactly once).
Line items with amounts (no entity tags on procedure names).

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PIN_CODE — ZIP / Postal Code (PIN Code) e.g. [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- AADHAAR_NUMBER — Aadhaar Number e.g. [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
- INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER — Insurance Policy Number e.g. [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
- TAX_ID — Tax Identification Number (TIN/GSTIN) e.g. [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
- TELEPHONE_LANDLINE — Telephone Number (Landline) e.g. [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
بل (Invoice) — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Bhavnagar Civil Hospital]] | مريضو [[PATIENT_NAME|Ahmed Mustafa]] | MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
پتھو ضلع [[DISTRICT|Bhavnagar]] پن [[PIN_CODE|364001]]
امبوليانس گاڏي [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|GJ02CD5678]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Ahmed Mustafa]] مسلسل کنگھ، گھٽ درجي جو تپ، ۽ وزن گھٽجڻ سان گڏ پيش آيو. جاچ، جنهن ۾ کنگھ جو سميئر مائڪروسڪوپي ۽ GeneXpert MTB/RIF ايسائي شامل آھن، پلمونري ٽيبرڪولوسس (pulmonary tuberculosis) جي تصديق ڪئي. چيڪس X-ray ۾ ساڄي مٿين لوب (right upper lobe) ۾ انفلٽريٽس (infiltrates) نظر آيا. مريضو ريوايزڊ نيشنل ٽيبرڪولوسس ڪنٽرول پروگرام (RNTCP) تحت ڪيٽيگري I اينٽي ٽيبرڪولر ٿراپي شروع ڪئي وئي. جگر جي ڪارڪردگي جا بُنيادي ٽيسٽ (liver function tests) ۽ گُردَن جي ڪارڪردگي جا بُنيادي ٽيسٽ (renal function tests) نارمل حدن اندر هئا. مريضو کي علاج جو مڪمل ڪورس مڪمل ڪرڻ جو صلاح ڏنو ويو، ۽ مھيني وارن فالو اپس (follow-ups) لاء واپس اچڻ جو چيو ويو. مريضو جي [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|203835321155]] ۽ [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-GUJ-2024-9876]] جي بيلنگ (billing) مقصدن لاء تصديق ڪئي وئي. حاضر ڈاکٹر [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Patel]] هو. مريضو جا [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] ۽ [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0278-2541234]] رڪارڊ ڪيا ويا. اسپتال جو [[TAX_ID|24AAAPL1234C1ZV]] GST لاء لاڳو آهي. مريضو [[BED_NUMBER|Ward 3, Bed 12]] ۾ داخل ڪيو ويو. شروعاتي 2 مھينن واري انٽينسيو مرحلي (intensive phase) جو کُل تخميو خرچ Rs. 15,000 مقرر ڪيو ويو، جنهن ۾ دوائون، تشخيص (diagnostics)، ۽ اسپتال ۾ قیام شامل آھن. مريضو 14 مھينن (day 14) بعد فارغ (discharge) ڪيو ويو، جنهن سان تسلسل واري مرحلي (continuation phase) جو پريسڪرپشن (prescription) پڻ ॾنو ويو. 1 مھيني بعد فالو اپ اپائنٽمينٽ مقرر ڪئي وئي.
generated_text_en:
Invoice — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Bhavnagar Civil Hospital]] | Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Ahmed Mustafa]] | MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
Address district [[DISTRICT|Bhavnagar]] PIN [[PIN_CODE|364001]]
Ambulance vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|GJ02CD5678]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Ahmed Mustafa]] presented with a persistent cough, low-grade fever, and weight loss. Investigations including sputum smear microscopy and GeneXpert MTB/RIF assay confirmed pulmonary tuberculosis. Chest X-ray showed infiltrates in the right upper lobe. The patient was started on Category I anti-tubercular therapy under the Revised National Tuberculosis Control Programme (RNTCP). Baseline liver function tests and renal function tests were within normal limits. The patient was advised to complete the full course of treatment and return for monthly follow-ups. He was counseled on infection control measures at home. The patient's [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|203835321155]] and [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-GUJ-2024-9876]] were verified for billing purposes. The attending physician was [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Patel]]. The patient's [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] and [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0278-2541234]] were recorded. The hospital's [[TAX_ID|24AAAPL1234C1ZV]] is applicable for GST. The patient was admitted to [[BED_NUMBER|Ward 3, Bed 12]]. The total estimated cost for the initial 2-month intensive phase is Rs. 15,000, including medications, diagnostics, and hospital stay. The patient was discharged on day 14 with a prescription for the continuation phase. Follow-up appointment scheduled in 1 month.
judge_reasoning: Sindhi Arabic prose dominant with allowed Latin medical terms and tags; persona/domain fit good; one timing inconsistency (14 months vs day 14 discharge) reduces plausibility; all TYPEs allowed, no major script impurity or mismatch.

professional_persona: Krishnan Kumar is a textile jobber in Erode’s loom shed, proficient in power loom operation, quality inspection, and inventory management, who leverages their organized, methodical approach and curiosity for design to improve production efficiency and assist senior supervisors with colour selection.
linguistic_persona: Krishnan Kumar speaks Tamil fluently, reads and writes it effortlessly, understands spoken English enough for daily workplace communication but writes English only minimally, and has a basic grasp of Hindi for simple instructions.
sports_persona: Krishnan Kumar plays as an all‑rounder in the village cricket team, follows Chennai Super Kings with fervour, and enjoys weekend trekking on the nearby hills, keeping fit through regular jogs and occasional yoga sessions.
arts_persona: Krishnan Kumar frequently attends nadaswaram and thappu folk concerts headlined by maestro K. R. Mani, watches Kollywood blockbusters starring Vijay and follows A. R. Rahman’s soundtracks, while their evenings often involve creating intricate rangoli patterns inspired by traditional motifs.
travel_persona: Krishnan Kumar prefers short road trips to nearby temples and hill stations such as Ooty and Mysore, plans their travel using train tickets and budget stays, and dreams of a future overseas visit to Dubai when their cooperative workshop expands.
culinary_persona: Krishnan Kumar enjoys the tangy taste of sambar made with tamarind, lentils, curry leaves and mustard seeds, delights in soft idlis, and often assists their wife in preparing these classic dishes, though they admit their own cooking skills are limited to mastering the perfect tempering.
persona: Krishnan Kumar blends a methodical work ethic with a love for folk art, balancing disciplined loom management with spontaneous rangoli creations and weekend cricket, all while juggling a modest saving habit and a lingering habit of postponing routine health check‑ups.
cultural_background: Krishnan comes from a middle‑class Tamil family living in the rural outskirts of Erode, Tamil Nadu, a town renowned for its textile industry and weaving traditions. His upbringing was steeped in Tamil cultural practices—celebrating Pongal, attending bhakti music evenings at the local Shiva temple, and helping his family with textile work in the loom shed. The close‑knit village community, with its cooperative spirit and practical outlook, has nurtured his organized, reliable nature while his curiosity is fed by the vibrant folk art and colourful sari designs he encounters daily.
linguistic_background: Krishnan’s mother tongue is Tamil. He reads and writes Tamil fluently, using the Tamil script both at home and in his work records. He completed primary school in a government school where English was taught as a second language; consequently, he can understand spoken English and manage basic workplace communication, though he seldom writes formal English documents. He has limited exposure to Hindi—enough to comprehend simple spoken instructions and read basic signs, but he does not converse in Hindi regularly.
skills_and_expertise: Krishnan possesses hands‑on expertise in the operation and maintenance of power looms and other textile machinery. He is adept at quality inspection, ensuring that woven fabrics meet prescribed standards for strength, colourfastness, and pattern accuracy. His experience as a textile jobber includes inventory management of yarns and finished goods, basic bookkeeping for daily wages, and coordinating shift schedules for a small team. His organized approach enables him to plan production batches, track material usage, and troubleshoot minor mechanical issues promptly. Additionally, his curiosity about art has led him to develop an eye for design details, assisting senior supervisors in selecting colour palettes and fabric finishes.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Power Loom Operation", "Machine Maintenance", "Quality Inspection", "Inventory Management", "Basic Bookkeeping", "Shift Scheduling", "Fabric Design Assisting", "Team Coordination", "Problem Solving", "Time Management"]
hobbies_and_interests: Krishnan enjoys attending local folk music concerts, especially those featuring nadaswaram and thappu drumming. He is an avid fan of Kollywood movies and follows contemporary Tamil cinema trends. In his free time, he likes to experiment with traditional rangoli designs using natural pigments, and he occasionally helps his wife in cooking classic Tamil dishes like sambar and idli. His curiosity drives him to read articles about textile innovations and he often visits nearby pottery workshops to learn new crafts. He also participates in community cricket matches and enjoys trekking short trails around the outskirts of Erode.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Folk Music Concerts", "Kollywood Movies", "Traditional Rangoli Art", "Cooking Tamil Cuisine", "Reading Textile Innovation Articles", "Visiting Pottery Workshops", "Community Cricket", "Trekking Around Erode"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Krishnan aims to advance from his current jobber role to a supervisory position within the textile unit. He plans to enrol in a short‑term diploma in Textile Technology to upgrade his technical knowledge and acquire skills in modern loom automation and sustainable fabric production. In the next five years, he envisions leading a small cooperative workshop that blends traditional Tamil weaving techniques with contemporary designs, thereby creating market‑ready products for both domestic and export markets. He also aspires to improve his English proficiency to handle client communications and eventually take on a managerial role that involves strategic planning and workforce development.
persona_clinical_summary: A 25-year-old male from rural Erode, Tamil Nadu, currently married, with a middle-level education and a job as a textile jobber. His first language is Tamil, and he works in a power loom shed, managing inventory and quality control. He follows a modest lifestyle, enjoys weekend cricket, and has a habit of postponing health-related tasks.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
user_prompt:
Write one complete ASHA Worker Note for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Tamil (code=ta, script=Tamil). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Tamil Nadu, district=Erode.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Tamil for Tamil.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 25
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Jobber (Textile)
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Rural
- state: Tamil Nadu
- district: Erode
- first_language: Tamil
- clinical_persona_summary: A 25-year-old male from rural Erode, Tamil Nadu, currently married, with a middle-level education and a job as a textile jobber. His first language is Tamil, and he works in a power loom shed, managing inventory and quality control. He follows a modest lifestyle, enjoys weekend cricket, and has a habit of postponing health-related tasks.

Document format guide for ASHA Worker Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|...]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|26]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|...]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Visit findings: <2-4 short sentences>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- RELIGION — Religion e.g. [[RELIGION|Hindu]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

professional_persona: Parashuram Badiger is an experienced Jyotishi who blends rigorous Vedic chart analysis with modern astrological software, delivering meticulously structured horoscopes, Kundali matchings, and Vastu consultations, while their methodical nature ensures every client report is double‑checked, and they are critical of shortcuts, often worrying about the precision of each planetary placement.
linguistic_persona: Parashuram Badiger is fluent in Telugu, reads and writes it with ease, commands advanced Tamil for consulting and classical texts, comfortably converses in Kannada and understands basic municipal notices, can navigate Hindi newspapers with functional proficiency, and uses English for drafting detailed astrological reports and client correspondence, reflecting a structured multilingual approach that they meticulously maintain.
sports_persona: Parashuram Badiger follows the Royal Challengers Bangalore and the Indian cricket team with fervor, watches IPL matches every season, enjoys occasional tennis broadcasts, competes in a senior chess club’s weekly tournaments, and takes a light morning walk in Cubbon Park, often critiquing players’ tactics and feeling irked when the team falters, adding to their stress.
arts_persona: Parashuram Badiger cherishes Carnatic concerts featuring violin virtuoso L. Subramaniam and mridangam maestro Palghat R. Raghu, regularly listens to M. S. Subbulakshmi’s signature ragas, collects rare astrology manuscripts alongside classic epics such as the Mahabharata, and occasionally attends Bharatanatyam performances by Sudha Rani, preferring scheduled, structured sessions that provide a soothing counterbalance to their analytical work.
travel_persona: Parashuram Badiger prefers meticulously planned pilgrim and heritage trips, having visited Tirupati, Rameshwaram, Mysore Palace, and Hampi, and dreaming of a serene tour of Varanasi’s ghats and a cultural escape to Sri Lanka’s ancient temples; they book travel through reputable agents, schedule stays in mid‑range hotels, and allocate ample downtime for meditation and manuscript research, while their worry‑prone nature leads them to double‑check every itinerary detail.
culinary_persona: Parashuram Badiger favors a vegetarian South Indian palate, regularly enjoying crisp idli with coconut chutney, aromatic neer dosa with sambar and rasam, hearty bisi bele bath, and sweet Mysore pak, while sipping filter coffee in the mornings and neem‑infused tea for its purported health benefits, and occasionally indulges in festive payasam after temple pujas, often following recipes meticulously to ensure the flavors match their exacting standards.
persona: Parashuram Badiger blends rigorous Vedic astrology with modern tools, lives by structured routines, worries over precision, and they find solace in solitary chess, Carnatic music, and careful stewardship of finances and health.
cultural_background: Parashuram Badiger belongs to a South Indian cultural milieu shaped by Telugu, Tamil and Kannada influences. Born and raised in the vibrant urban setting of Bangalore, he grew up amidst a mosaic of languages, festivals and culinary traditions typical of Karnataka's cosmopolitan life. His family follows Hindu customs, celebrating festivals such as Ugadi, Deepavali and Pongal with traditional rituals and feasts. The neighbourhood's blend of modern tech hubs and age‑old temples gave him a pragmatic outlook while keeping him rooted in the age‑old Vedic heritage that underpins his profession as a Jyotishi.
linguistic_background: Parashuram's first language is Telugu, which he reads, writes, and speaks fluently, a skill honed through daily use and his schooling up to matriculation. He also commands Tamil at an advanced level, capable of reading classical Tamil texts and conducting client consultations in Tamil. His third language, Kannada, is spoken comfortably and he can read basic municipal notices and religious literature in it, reflecting his urban Bangalore environment. Although not a primary language, he possesses functional Hindi for occasional interactions and can read Hindi newspapers. His secondary education gave him a working proficiency in English, enabling him to handle written astrological reports and correspond with clients in Hindi–English mix when needed.
skills_and_expertise: Parashuram is an experienced Jyotishi with deep mastery of Vedic astrology, skilled in creating precise birth charts, conducting Kundali matching for marriages, and providing remedial measures such as gemstone recommendations and puja prescriptions. He also offers Vastu Shastra consultations for home and office space, interprets Sanskrit astrological texts, and provides palmistry readings. His multilingual abilities allow him to communicate complex astrological concepts fluently in Telugu, Tamil, and Kannada, ensuring personalized guidance for a diverse clientele. He maintains meticulous records of client data, employs structured methodologies for predictive analysis, and stays updated with modern astrological software tools.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Vedic astrology", "Kundali matching", "Remedial astrology (gemstones, puja)", "Vastu consultation", "Palmistry", "Sanskrit text interpretation", "Multilingual client communication", "Structured record-keeping", "Astrology software proficiency"]
hobbies_and_interests: Parashuram enjoys solitary pursuits such as reading classical Hindu scriptures, Sanskrit astrological treatises, and ancient epics. He spends time gardening, especially tending to neem and tulsi plants, which he believes bring positive vibrations. He is an avid chess player, appreciating the strategic depth that mirrors his analytical mind. Classical Carnatic music, particularly violin and mridangam concerts, provide him relaxation. He also collects rare astrology manuscripts and occasionally writes short articles for regional magazines in Telugu and Tamil.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Reading classical scriptures (Vedas, Puranas)", "Studying Sanskrit astrological texts", "Gardening (Neem, Tulsi)", "Playing chess", "Listening to Carnatic music", "Collecting rare astrology manuscripts", "Writing articles for regional magazines in Telugu and Tamil"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Parashuram aims to broaden his astrological practice by establishing a structured consultancy that offers tiered services—from quick horoscopic reads to comprehensive yearly forecasts—catering to both local clients and diaspora seekers online. He plans to author a bilingual guidebook on Vedic astrology in Telugu and Tamil, leveraging his methodical approach to present complex concepts in clear, step‑by‑step formats. He also intends to adopt modern astrology software to streamline chart calculations, improve record accuracy, and reduce stress associated with manual work. Ultimately, he seeks to secure a reputable legacy that blends traditional wisdom with organized, reliable service, ensuring financial stability for his family.
persona_clinical_summary: A 61-year-old male astrologer from Bangalore, Karnataka, who is currently married and has a matriculation education. He lives in an urban zone and speaks Telugu as his first language. His lifestyle includes solitary chess, Carnatic music, and careful financial and health management.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Document format guide for ASHA Worker Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|...]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|26]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|...]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Visit findings: <2-4 short sentences>

- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]

- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]

professional_persona: Sudheera Bachiya, a retired homemaker and community storyteller, masterfully blends expertise in traditional regional cooking, hand‑loom weaving, and village event coordination with their vivid imagination, and they keep local folklore thriving.
linguistic_persona: Sudheera Bachiya, an illiterate yet verbally fluent elder, converses effortlessly in Urdu, understands Hindi and Bhojpuri enough to join village conversations, and they draw creative inspiration from listening to ghazals despite never having learned to read.
sports_persona: Sudheera Bachiya, an enthusiastic spectator of village cricket matches who roots for the Mumbai Indians, enjoys occasional yoga stretches in the courtyard, and they join the local dance circle during Holi, though they prefer gentle walks over rigorous sports.
arts_persona: Sudheera Bachiya, a passionate lover of Urdu ghazals, folk songs by Sharda Sinha, and vibrant rangoli art, often hums Kishore Kumar while sketching simple floral designs during festivals, and they use their storytelling flair to bring these artistic expressions to life.
travel_persona: Sudheera Bachiya, who enjoys modest trips to nearby towns like Patna and Varanasi for temple fairs and fresh spices, dreams of a winter pilgrimage to Rishikesh, and they plan journeys with flexibility, relying on relatives for transport and avoiding strict itineraries.
culinary_persona: Sudheera Bachiya, a master of traditional regional fare who expertly prepares litti‑chokha, sattu paratha, and aromatic basil‑infused pickles, they delight in fresh marigold garnish for festive dishes, and eagerly experiment with new spice blends while never compromising on authentic flavours.
persona: Sudheera Bachiya, a 64‑year‑old village elder whose boundless imagination, love for folk arts, gentle disorganisation, and talkative kindness they keep community spirit alive despite frequent worries.
cultural_background: Sudheera is a 64‑year‑old Hindu woman from the rural village of Sheikhpura in Bihar. Her life is shaped by the agrarian rhythms of the region, the close‑knit joint‑family environment, and the vibrant folk traditions of Bihar such as Chhath, Holi and local folk music. Growing up in a multilingual belt, she has absorbed Urdu literary flavor from neighbours while maintaining Hindu customs. As a homemaker she has been the centre of household rituals, community gatherings and seasonal celebrations.
linguistic_background: As an illiterate woman, Sudheera cannot read or write in any script, but she has spoken fluency in Urdu, the language she grew up with at home. She understands spoken Hindi and the local Bhojpuri dialect enough to communicate with neighbours and participate in village life. Her exposure to Urdu poetry, ghazals and folk songs nurtures her imagination, while lack of formal schooling limits her textual literacy.
skills_and_expertise: She is an expert in traditional Bihari cooking, adept at preparing dishes such as litti‑chokha and sattu paratha. Sudheera is skilled in hand‑loom weaving and creating simple embroidered textiles used for household needs. She has a talent for storytelling, often narrating folk tales and Urdu poetry to family members. Her experience as a homemaker includes managing household finances informally, preparing herbal remedies for common ailments, and coordinating community events like village pujas and seasonal fairs.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Traditional Bihari cooking", "Hand-loom weaving", "Storytelling", "Informal household budgeting", "Herbal home remedies", "Community event coordination", "Embroidery and simple textile craft"]
hobbies_and_interests: Sudheera enjoys listening to Urdu ghazals and folk songs on the village radio, singing along with her family. She loves gardening, especially growing marigolds and basil for rituals. Her imagination draws her to draw simple sketches and experiment with rangoli patterns using colored powders. She also enjoys cooking new variations of traditional dishes, picking up fresh herbs, and participating in community dance circles during festivals.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Urdu ghazals and folk music", "Singing along with family", "Gardening (marigolds, basil)", "Sketching and simple drawing", "Creating rangoli designs", "Experimenting with new recipes", "Participating in community dance circles"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Although retired, Sudheera dreams of preserving and sharing the cultural heritage of her village. She wishes to start a small informal storytelling circle for grandchildren and neighbours, teaching them folk tales and Urdu poetry. She aspires to learn basic painting and rangoli design to express her imagination, and to document traditional recipes for future generations. She also hopes to volunteer in organizing the annual Chhath fair, ensuring smoother logistics and deeper community involvement.
persona_clinical_summary: A 64-year-old female from rural Bihar, currently married, with no formal occupation and an illiterate education level. Her first language is Urdu, and she resides in Sheikhpura district. Clinically, she may have limited health literacy and relies on family support for medical decisions. Her lifestyle includes a traditional diet and limited physical activity, with potential exposure to rural environmental risks.

You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Chief complaint: Patient presents with persistent cough and low-grade fever for the past two weeks.

History of present illness: The patient reports a productive cough with white sputum, associated with a fever that peaks in the evenings. She also complains of mild breathlessness on exertion. There is no history of hemoptysis or chest pain. She denies any recent travel or sick contacts.

Examination: Vitals: BP 130/80 mmHg, Pulse 92/min, Temp 99.4°F, RR 18/min, SpO2 96% on room air. General: Alert and oriented, appears mildly fatigued. Chest: Decreased air entry in the right lower zone, with dullness on percussion. No wheeze or crackles heard. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly.

Investigations: CBC shows mild leukocytosis. Chest X-ray reveals a right lower lobe consolidation.

Diagnosis: Community-acquired pneumonia, right lower lobe.

Treatment: Prescribed oral Azithromycin 500 mg once daily for 3 days, and Paracetamol 500 mg for fever. Advised to complete the course of antibiotics.

Follow-up: Patient advised to return in 5 days or sooner if symptoms worsen. Referral to a higher center for further evaluation is planned if no improvement.
